items.question_text: According to 4-5-8, when should a controller inform an aircraft about when to expect a climb or descent clearance?
items.explanation: The paragraph states: if practicable, inform an aircraft when to expect climb or descent clearance or to request altitude change from another facility. The 'if practicable' qualifier allows controllers to prioritize other duties.

choices.text: Only when the pilot specifically requests that information.
If practicable.
Always, regardless of workload.
Only when the altitude change exceeds 4,000 feet.

items.question_text: What phraseology may be used to tell an aircraft when to expect a higher altitude at a specific fix?
items.explanation: The phraseology includes 'EXPECT HIGHER/LOWER IN (number of miles or minutes) MILES/MINUTES, or AT (fix).' For a fix-based expectation, the phrase uses AT.

choices.text: Expect higher after passing the fix.
Expect higher at (fix).
Climb when reaching (fix) at pilot discretion.
Altitude change available beyond (fix).

items.question_text: Under 4-6-5, a visual holding point may be used as a holding fix only if what condition is met regarding the pilot?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-6-5 states you may use a location the pilot can determine by visual reference to the surface if he/she is familiar with it. Pilot familiarity is the key condition.

choices.text: The pilot is on an IFR flight plan.
The pilot is familiar with the location.
The location is charted on a sectional.
The pilot has a GPS waypoint confirming the position.

items.question_text: When an aircraft is holding at an unmonitored NAVAID, from what must ATC separate it?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-6-7 requires separating an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID from any other aircraft occupying the course the holding aircraft will follow if it does not receive signals from the NAVAID. This protects against the holding aircraft drifting off the prescribed pattern.

choices.text: Any other aircraft occupying the course the holding aircraft will follow if NAVAID signals are lost.
All aircraft within 30 NM of the NAVAID.
Only aircraft holding at the same NAVAID on the opposite side.
Only aircraft below the holding altitude.

items.question_text: What is the required timing for transferring radio communications and control responsibility under 4-7-9?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-9 states: transfer radio communications and control responsibility early enough to allow the receiving facility to clear an aircraft beyond the clearance limit before the aircraft reaches it.

choices.text: At the clearance limit boundary.
Early enough to allow the receiving facility to clear the aircraft beyond the clearance limit before the aircraft reaches it.
Any time before the aircraft reaches the destination airport.
Only after the aircraft has reported level at the clearance limit.

items.question_text: When issuing an approach clearance at an airport without an operating control tower, what must the controller include?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-8-2 requires stating the name of the airport when issuing an approach clearance where there is no operating control tower or where a part-time tower is closed.

choices.text: The name of the approach procedure only.
The name of the airport.
The CTAF frequency for the airport.
The FSS serving the airport.

items.question_text: When relaying an approach clearance through a communication station other than an air carrier company radio, what must be included when required and available?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-8-3 requires including the weather report when it is required and available. The controller may accomplish this by telling the station to issue current weather.

choices.text: The current weather report.
The runway condition report.
The NOTAMs for the destination.
The approach plate effective date.

items.question_text: What altitudes may be specified for military high altitude instrument approaches when required for separation?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-8-4 states altitudes above those shown on the high altitude instrument approach procedures chart may be specified when required for separation. However, a note warns that maximum altitudes are determined through coordination with the military authority to avoid exceeding rate-of-descent or airspeed limitations.

choices.text: Any altitude the controller determines is safe.
Altitudes above those shown on the high altitude instrument approach procedures chart.
Only altitudes shown on the approach chart.
Altitudes below the published minimums when traffic permits.

items.question_text: If practicable, inform an aircraft when to expect climb or descent clearance, or when to request an altitude change from another facility.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: This is the directive of paragraph 4-5-8, qualified by 'if practicable.'

items.situation: A pilot holding in a published pattern requests a deviation from the prescribed holding flight path to avoid building cumulus clouds. The deviation would keep the aircraft clear of all known obstacles and no other traffic is on the holding course.
items.para_context: Approve a pilot's request to deviate from the prescribed holding flight path if obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

choices.text: Yes, because obstacles and traffic conditions permit the deviation.
No, because holding patterns are never deviated from.
No, the pilot should cancel IFR and navigate around weather VFR.
Yes, but only after the pilot declares an emergency.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-6-6 directs approval of pilot-requested holding flight path deviations when obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

items.instruction: Select the correct separation requirement for an unmonitored NAVAID hold.
items.question_text: Which separation action is specifically required by 4-6-7 for an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID?

choices.text: Separate the holding aircraft from other aircraft occupying the course it would follow if NAVAID signals are lost.
Separate the holding aircraft from all aircraft within the NAVAID service volume.
Separate the holding aircraft from aircraft at adjacent flight levels only.
No special separation is required if the aircraft is in radar contact.

items.explanation: The rule specifically requires separation from any other aircraft occupying the course the holding aircraft will follow if it does not receive signals from the NAVAID.

items.instruction: Select the correctly formatted approach clearance.
items.question_text: Which approach clearance format is correct for an uncontrolled airport?

choices.text: Cleared for the approach.
Cleared ILS approach to Centerville airport.
Cleared ILS runway 27 approach.
Cleared approach, airport is Centerville.

items.explanation: At an airport without an operating control tower, the airport name must be stated: 'Cleared (type) Approach to (airport name).'

items.question_text: For military high altitude instrument approaches, the maximum assignable altitudes must be determined through coordination between the ATC facility and the military authority to avoid exceeding rate-of-descent or airspeed limitations.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note to 4-8-4 states maximum altitudes are determined through coordination with the military authority specifically to prevent exceeding rate-of-descent or airspeed limitations.
